speaker: interviewee
text: I always have to do something with my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape's up.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2, September 15, 1995, interview with Gizel Berman. Can you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I should tell you about a very special year I had the good fortune to experience. My mother, as always, she wanted to be sure that I don't miss on anything. And she felt, oh, I should have a better finishing-- it was called a finishing school. But I'm ashamed, actually, to call it-- and I will just call it a French-speaking school in Brussels. I did have the relatives. That made it much more pleasant. And I was about 17 years old and just finished my business college. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you state the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Institute [FRENCH]. And I knew that I'll have a chance to take some classes in craft. And anyway, I don't know, it seemed to be quite easy. It's true, too, that that was the same time that my boyfriend just left for America. And so that made it much easier that-- after we were friends for five, six years, and daily visits-- because we lived next to each other-- and daily walks together. But that's it, of course. And still, once he left, I just didn't mind to go to France-- I mean, to Belgium. The unusual story part was that-- where we-- I had to travel through Germany. And in '38 already, Germany was, was much more known about the things what's happening there. And, and I was a little bit ill at ease. But because I did speak German fluently, it didn't bother me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I traveled one day, one night all alone. And when I arrived to Brussels, the relatives were very kind. They were very distant relatives but very nice and kind. And it made me feel very good because right away, it was established that every Sunday I have dinner with them in their home. And the school was-- it was a madhouse. It's a-- that's when I found out what, what is about lesbians. I, I, I didn't even know then what it is until much later, when I realized that people would point at each other and-- how come you let her to sleep with you in the bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, it's just friendship. And one foreign girl and the other one, they want some warmness or intimacy, so ask to sleep with each-- together in the bed, sharing the bed. Anyway, so I learned that. And I learned that-- it's nothing wrong. We, we got along beautifully. I mean, most girls were already having sex life for years before their not-so-smart parents send them away and lock them up at this all-girls school. And they went crazy, so affection here, affection there. So they got to be good to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, it, it, it made all sense. I understood it once I realized how they lived before and what it's about. And there was from every country. And it was really interesting, really, really fascinating. And I couldn't tell what was the best part or the worst. I had no problem with anyone. And it went fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was trying their very best. They were also to kind of keep us on a leash. Because they knew it from previous experiences, the girls who come there, they are not exactly little girls. They are young woman. And here, they have to live in a different life. So they didn't do much help. Meantime, the food was fantastic. And we indulged eating, like, unreal. Every breakfast, what we ate-- with, with chocolate sauce and the butter and honey. Well, we just ate. So, as a good-- as a proof of it, when I got home a year later, I gained 40 pounds. And I shouldn't forget it that the first sentence from my mother was, my child, I sent you to acquire the French language. But what I see, you certainly acquired lots of pounds. So she was very unhappy. And she was right. I did learn the French language quite easily. And I really liked it. We, we did things which we would have never done and had skating lessons with ex-Russian aristocrats who were during the White Russian times. And they were now in bad financial shape. And everybody had a title of some kind. And I was learning how to dance on the ice. Well, anyway. It was nice. I could have lived without it. It was fine. I did make a resolution-- if I ever would have a child, I would never send to a all-girls finishing school because it really could finish you up. And then one day, we went out to Holland on a-- for a-- the tulip season, to observe. And I said, gee, tulip season. Hmm, it might rain. I remembered that I just had the brand-new, red plastic coat. We just came out in Czechoslovakia. And my mother bought it. And I had a little red beret, mini-beret, made for it. Anyway, I was all dressed up. And we went to-- by train to Holland. And it was gorgeous and really, a beautiful experience, those flowers. Of course I said, gee, tomorrow, I'm going to visit my relatives. That should be nice to buy some flowers for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought a big bouquet of red tulips. And here I was, going now home. And I said, I just should keep it fresh so tomorrow everything should look fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, I get dressed in my previous day outfit and took the bus. I had to change once, you know, from the school to go to the-- my relatives. I was very excited because I was carrying a beautiful bouquet of flowers. And what happens while I'm crossing from one bus to the other one-- suddenly, I see a huge mob of people come up from the side street, just tremendous. I wasn't scared. But I was very surprised. I had no idea what could-- what's going on, why. I just mind my business. And I was crossing. And in no time, two men grabbed me on-- by each arm. And they put me right in the front row. And they talked to me good Flemish, didn't understand a word. Then they talked French. And I understand very little. But I did understand that it's 1st of May, and how nice for me to be dressed so nicely up for the 1st of May. And I thought, I'll die. Then I got scared since nobody will know what happened to me. And I started to beg them. And I got all my French and everything-- didn't do it. They say, you have to walk with us. There's just no two way. You are our, well, main decor for the 1st of May. And don't complain and this. And I started to cry. And that didn't help. Anyway, I was walking with them for about four hours. I mean, not walking, they were dragging me, pulling me. And after about four hours-- and I probably was numb; I couldn't cry or say [? a peep-- ?] they said, now, OK, you can go. And I had no idea where. Luckily, I had money with me. I got a taxi. And I, I gave him the address to my relatives. And I arrived there, like, five hours later. It was evening by the time I arrived. And I must have looked like hell because they just almost fainted at taking a look at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it turned out, yes, it was the 1st of May parade. And that was my big honor that I was walking in the front row with them, with the workers. And they called up the school. And the school was very understanding. I can sleep overnight. So I was at least compensated for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the tulips didn't look like tulips. They were just garbage. They just was ruined because I squeezed it so hard. And my tears probably killed it. So that was one of those nice little outings for-- in Brussels. And then, when the time came to kind of go home, I was very fearful. Because by that time, the Russian-- the Germans' reputation changed a great deal in the papers-- what's going on in one country, the other one. I says, I can't bear to go to-- to travel to Germany in the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that's why, in the last minute, I decided I will run over to a store, bought myself a yard of ivory-colored silk. I had paints because I always had. And I, like, in a couple hours, I painted myself a scarf with maybe 20 different kind of flags. And one of them I put on was the Hakenkreuz, which is-- what is it in English? A swastika.

speaker: interviewee
text: A swastika, that's right. And once I did it, I put it down on the silk, I was so relieved. I just looked at it like, oh, wonderful, wonderful. Sure enough, I got dressed the next day. And I put the scarf around my neck. And the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Swastika.

speaker: interviewee
text: --swastika was right underneath my chin. And I sat in the, the railroad train like, hmm. I just wondered, should I speak if somebody talks to me or shouldn't speak? Well, they all came in. They all clicked their heels very strongly and politely. And they were all highly polished and miserable looking, scary looking-- not miserable, scary looking. I mean, they had a look in them that-- well, I tried not to speak. I didn't say I know anything in English or French. I just sat like a dummy with my little Hakenkreuz. And the country looked terrible. All over during the night, there were just flames from every factory. And it was fearful. Well, once I got back to Czechoslovakia, I was very, very relieved. That wasn't even so simple because my mother called me up-- I should have come home, like, two weeks earlier already than I went. And she says, it's dangerous. Come home as soon as possible. And I went to discuss it with the head lady mistress, Mrs.-- I can't think right now-- doesn't matter. And she came next day to the classroom and said, kids, I have a sad story. And she talked for a few minutes. And I had no idea that it was about me. There is somebody in great danger amongst us here. And I want you to all take it to your heart and think it over after I tell you what it's about and speak to you parents. We should help her. She shouldn't have to go back to Czechoslovakia-- and see how can we help her to keep her here. And I really almost passed out. And I just-- anyway, so she put out this SOS for me. And next day, about four girls-- one was a-- the Turkish one was the first one. And there was the English one. There was a Hindu, an Indian one, and somebody else who offered-- they had to get in contact with their parents. And this wasn't exactly easy. And I thanked them very profusely and everything. And I said, nope, I'm going home. My mother wants me home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I said, when I came home-- by that time, it wasn't the same Europe. It was a very changed atmosphere. And my mother started to give me lectures. Keep in mind we are Jewish. Don't be conspicuous. Don't speak loudly. Don't behave in any different, unbecoming manners. And I said, mother, you told this to me before, too. So why would I do it now? She said, no, you have to be super careful. Anything can happen the way things are. So I-- it really sunk in with me very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right when I came home, my brother, the-- my younger brother, left. He was all ready to go. But he was waiting until I come home. And he left with one of the first illegal ships to Palestine. He was 18 years old, a real, dedicated Zionist. He wanted to help Palestine and work there. He wasn't equipped with no knowledge whatsoever. And I missed him immensely. Because he and I got along beautifully.

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother and I, we didn't because we each had the top position. He was the first child and the first boy. And I was the only girl. And we just had, like, a competition going. Why you, and why not me? So anyway, we got along but not as well as I got along with my other brother. So Europe was completely changed. And, and my mother was very concerned, still, about more learning. And she says, you are-- oh yeah, meantime, I proved to my mother that I can sew real well because I took some sewing-- still before I went to Brussels. I was extremely good at it. And so when I came back, she says, look, you have such talent for that. Wouldn't you like to go and learn some designing in Budapest? I say, sure, mother. So I went to a very snazzy place for six weeks. By that time, I knew Nick. And so he even came up-- What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Had to be '38, '39, probably '39 and-- in between. So I went to Budapest to learn this designing. And I enjoyed it because really, I really had a good feeling for it. And whatever my mother thought I should learn, I want to learn. But meantime, I started to be interested in boys-- men. And that was a brand new thing because I really never paid attention to men. I had this one childhood boyfriend. And I was 100% content. It was-- we were like brother, sisters, living side by side, and hoping that one day, maybe-- not-- yeah, we were really hoping we were [? mates. ?] So-- but he wasn't back in Czechoslovakia anymore. And that's the first occasion I met Nick. I-- well, there's a fun part to that encounter because the fellow who introduced us was a friend, kind of, mine-- an attorney. And he knew Nick. And after he introduced us, and he pulled me to the side-- kind of funny fellow-- he says, listen. That's a very good, marriageable material, I mean, of Nick-- candidate, OK, not material.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, who cares? I said, don't talk like that. You don't know. Dr. Nicholas Berman is a very, very popular catch, or something like that. It was very strongly recommended. I said, hmm, and anyway, nothing. But once I met him, came-- met my girlfriends, you know what? So I told them, guess what? Whom did I meet? Dr. Nick Berman. They said, oh, did you really? How is he? I said, well, I tell you, he's sure different from the ones we know. He's really a mature, intelligent, grown-- I said, very impressive, very impressive-- maybe not so much fun, I don't know, but impressive. And so the two girls told their parents each that, guess what? Gizel [NON-ENGLISH] met Nick Berman. And the mothers said, how come Gizel? Why don't you meet him, too? He's considered the best catch.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it wasn't a nice way to speak. But the fact was that everybody got a-- not marking-- grading, OK, where, like, I was considered the best third catch in the city, according money. He was not because of money, because he, he was a very successful doctor, prominent, and all these things. So the girls were all picked out. Because when they came on the conversation item, they talked to them about like merchandise. And I remember, actually, as a young girl-- one doctor from Prague, since I was age 12, would come home. That was still in Sobrance. He would come over, look me over like you look a piece of merchandise, like a horse or a cow in the market. He would touch me, my shoulder, and say, you look fine. Hey, you are growing, nice, nice. And I would tell him, listen, I'm not for sale. So you don't need to touch. Just tell me those nice words. I understand you. Turned out, he made up his mind when-- don't know at what age, maybe 10-- when I was 10 that he will wait for me because I was such a good catch. And he liked the family-- and prominent, and this. And he was actually a professor in Prague. That's even more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my mother liked him. And he liked my mother. Until one day, I said to my mother and him, I said, listen. Stop coming to look me over, OK? I don't want a-- you to look me over. If you see me on street, you say hello. But don't come over to visit me. You are just looking at me like a market-- piece of something for sale.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then, the three girls, we got together one day and says, listen. Now, turned out, meantime, they met Nick, too. And now we all knew him. And like, we were all in the race, kind of, fun, fun race.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we said-- I don't know whose idea. Could be easily mine. But I don't want to take this credit, or-- sounds like me. How about let's make a bet-- who will Nick Berman take first, from the three of us, to a movie? Good, good! What shall we bet? Well, the only big, luxury item was the few silk stockings from Austria. And they were, like, $25 would be today, a pair. And so we bet in three-- for three pair of pure silk stockings. So one would-- two of us has to pay it, and whatever, so it wouldn't be so high.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the bet was made, next day I called up Nick-- and meantime, I was a patient of his, meantime. Don't ask me why. I know why. I had two uncles. And they were both doing dentistry. And I couldn't stand them, two older gentlemen who always, all the-- kind of, look, oh, you look so good. You're growing up, yes. I say, yes, I'm growing up. And I said to my mother, I want to go to a strange dentist who will-- who will give me advice and not uncle this and uncle that. She says, OK, my child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I went to Dr. Berman. So now we knew each other officially as patient and then doctor. And what I did-- that called him up and told him I have something very important to discuss. Could we see each other for a short visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. He came to pick me up. His office and our house was very close. And we went for a walk. And we walked, like, at least two, three miles. Because we were good-- yeah, eventually we went on lots of hikes because we both were athletic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he talked, talked, talked, talked. And he came back. It was dark. And he said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH], I-- you never asked me what you wanted. I said, sorry, it's too late. Maybe tomorrow?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, sure, yes. I want to know what you want. So he went home. I went home. And next day he came back. And I said, now, let me start out what it was about. We made a bet. And this is the bet and told him the whole story. I couldn't care less. They really don't mean to me, all this. Nothing was so deadly serious. A man, a date, I don't know why. It's just didn't mean that--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, oh, I'll be delighted to take you to the movie. So sure enough, that same week, he took me to the movie. I got the, the stockings. The two girls just were furious. And the parents even more so. And I said, what's such a big deal, the stockings or Nick Berman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just realized that Nick is really different. He's not a boy. He's a man. And he treats me like a young lady. And so I had to smarten up and give up with my funny little joking ways. But I stay joking anyway. But we hit it off beautifully. We just hit it off beautifully. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You eventually married him and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And I wonder if you could just tell us a little bit about that and then how the shape of things changed in Uzhorod.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the life just got worse and worse and worse for Jewish people. And, and there were more and more restrictions and more no-nos. And oh god, my mother wouldn't let me-- I-- when I was 18, the-- anyway, they-- my mother indulged me, fur coat and all kinds of things. And then she put a stop sign-- you cannot wear this and this. And I want you just to blend in with the rest of the population. I don't want you to, to wear anything what is-- how do I say what-- catches-- eye-catching or something. And I learned it. I learned it very fast that it's a different world. And meantime, my relative kept bugging me, Gizel, you are now at a age. You are a marriageable age. Your mother is a good-looking young woman. And she's now alone for so many years. You should get married so she can get married. She said she's not going to get married until she has a marriageable daughter in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to state what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was 16 when he got a stroke and just died in couple hours. He had high blood pressure. And that was a big blow. That was a big blow because everything changed. Everything changed. I mean, the whole thinking-- but I went afterwards away to school, to Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother just tried very hard to, to still indulge me in as much good things as she could. But at the same time, she's very demanding on my behavior and my handling people and friendship. She kept a good eye on me. Do you want to say a couple words about your actual wedding day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a war wedding. [LAUGHS] Really, I don't know. We don't even agree on it, my husband and me, that how was it. It, it, it was-- [SIGH] I don't know. You know, I did everything, like, in a joke. Don't ask me why-- the way I met him, the way we got married. Meantime, dozens of matchmakers would be coming to the house. Some man from Switzerland came because he saw some picture. It was a real business. It was really like market with human flesh. And I couldn't stand it. And I just got furious all the-- all about it. And I really wanted to solve it, in a way, but not really by marriage, either. I didn't crave to be married. I just wanted to kind of live a little bit like a grownup. But when somebody, my relative, put tremendous pressure on me about marriage and I would talk to Nick about it, and we would be joking. I said, somebody came-- a matchmaker-- somebody came to meet me. And it was-- it was, to me, really, like, not very much to my liking. It really wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened, as I said, that one day, I got-- a wire or whatever came from Romania. My father's sister said, Gizel has to come right away to Romania to visit us. She-- there's a Jewish prince here. And she can't miss that occasion. It's just very, very special. So I made a joke. And I said to my mother, mother, do I need to? I couldn't care less. I really like Nick Berman. We get along beautifully. My child, but how can you hurt your aunt? To make the story short, I went out on my evening walk with Nick. We just walked, holding hands and walk, very square people. And so I said, guess what? I think I might have to go next week to Romania. He said, why? I said, they drive me crazy, another matchmaking deal. He said, for what is that? So he thought, he said, don't be silly. I'll marry you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, if you'll marry me, then I don't go to Romania. I said, good. I went home. I said to mother, no, it's all solved, mother, no more trips, no more matchmaking. Thank you-- finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married on what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 23, '41. But it, it was-- it was, like, a day before the wedding, we had a home wedding. And I remembered something that I lied to my-- to Nick. We were really in love with each other. But because of the war years, you didn't let yourself to be in love. It was too serious thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I said to him, you have to come. I have an emergency little discussion to have with you. I could just hear, like, his face just change.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came over in a half an hour later, and looked very strange on the settee. I says, I bet you he thinks I'm going to tell him that I'm not a virgin. And he'll be very surprised that's not what I'm telling. You know, I was having this discussion with myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he comes in. He says, well, what is it? So I said-- I said, well, listen. I lied for a whole lifetime about my age because my grandfather, from age 3, taught me you should lie about your age. Never tell your full age. So actually, I'm two years older. So I ended up being 22. And Nick was 32. And he was thrilled. And-- [LAUGHS] and so-- but little of crazinesses.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was always kind of very healthy because the times were very bad. We-- everybody was going to slave labor camps, my brother, my relatives, all people. And some didn't come back. And some-- just came the news, they are dying. Suddenly, it, it wasn't a normal life. From '38 on, it wasn't a normal life. We were Hungarians by that time. We even changed nationality. And that was lousy, very different from the Czechs or the Slovaks. But I just looked at it kind of, tried to do the best. And I said, how lucky. I have a wonderful friend. And we are really having respect for each other and on a very different level than all these other acquaintances. And so we were very appreciative that we found each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect.
